item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tone feels very casual. It reminds me of a Series I skip. I suggest a rewrite to make the tone more scientific

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one -

Commentary on the internet, a kind of "If Google wasn't 'not evil' but rather 'actually good'" thing going for it, which incidentally let's this borrow a lot from the mere existence of Dostoevsky (specifically The Idiot), fun little jabs at NYC falling apart and Telecoms being problematic.

The only parts that made me stop for a second where the neutrino thing, because I have no idea what the phrase is even supposed to mean and techno-babel makes me zone out, but mainly the 36 hour closing of a street in NYC. Is this a thing that can happen? Over a car accident? I've never been so I dunno, but that seems a little hard to believe. But hell, I live in the DFW, we have a totally different world vis traffic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Will someone put a title to the SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: WiFi Monoliths? An interesting concept. I'd say this article could use a bit of cleaning up and general revision, but I like it so far. Neutral vote pending any subsequent edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what should this SCP be named

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, very nice article, loved it.

BUT the object class is really, really wrong in my opinion. It's tagged with "uncontained" which would make it, at least, keter wouldn't it? As keter is "extremely, very much so, difficult to contain"???

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent series of edits by LordBlaze was reverted, as it unnecessarily changed the UK spelling variants of words to the US versions.

As noted in the post above, UK spelling and usage is acceptable on the wiki, as long as it is consistent. Editors should not be making edits to change UK spellings to US without permission.

item.title: Person Named Incorrectly
item.content:encoded:
Should the other person on the site in the chat log really be referred to as D-1493? Because, and correct me if I'm wrong, wouldn't that imply that this was a D-class personnel, under foundation custody? So the context of this conversation wouldn't make sense. I suggest we just change the description to the following:

Chat log between Researcher Nguyen and [put some sort of username here], a repeated user of SCP-3993-22. Conversation conducted on █████, a popular social media platform.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't try to stop quality critisisim, it's a legitimate issue with the article and he never said the article as a whole was bad

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: very interesting idea. i feel like the first paragraph of the description could be made better, but +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly what caught me best and kept it going until the end was
+ Ramblings
- Peace

how these got there, why, and how they were there to stay. You took the time to make these things legitimately Euclid and bear weight (no pun intended) in the article. I had to +1 because of the extents you went to make these things, again, Euclid and how the details in their containment procedures were written out to almost say, "Yes, we tried everything to get rid of these or stop their effect." Very good. That and (I'm not one who succumbs to this fear) you wrote the new-age, coming fear of technological assimilation very well and almost made it real. The symbolism of Wi-Fi replacing pay-phones was exceptionally clever.

This also actually shows the limiations (the cutting/destruction material used in suburban environments) and lengths (literally, covering-up thousands of these things with whatever band-aids are available) The Foundation had and will go to get something done. I'm simply impressed with how simple this idea is, yet well written under site standards. Kudos.

My one thing is, wouldn't surrounding structures, including sidewalks and asphalt, be under stress or even unable to support these things given how dense they are? I'd at least expect cracking in concrete from these things wherever they were put. But I'm nitpicking hard on that one. I suppose if an article is otherwise flawless, I'm going to see the only blemishes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree, no vote one way or the other though. Despite the flawed structure, it is still an interesting scp, and I appreciate hat it isn't an openly hostile or threatening scp. The real world references definitely need revisement or censoring (whichever would work, i have no practice in writing), and a less story based structure, unless you intend to add an after incident report.

On the plus side the scp itself is interesting, and a nice break from the monsters and brain melting abominations to reality. It works in a creepy factor as well, and subtly. Altruism, without any kind of compensation, is suspicious to people, it is widely believed that strangers don't do public works unless they want something. And the tagline at the end about it being only the beginning helps.

In short, good premise, subtle threat, but poor structure and pacing. Needs a solid buildup to ending threat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Note from Principal Researcher Macmillan: New Yorkers might not expect SCP-3993's physical interfaces to work, but we'll have to come up with something else for other cities."

+1 just for that. I'd give another for the whole "2017: A WiFi Odyssey" angle too if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral vote.

I feel that although this skip does what it does well, the idea/anomaly doesn't do it for me, along with finding the build-up a bit too slow, I don't see the story very well here. I can understand how the monolith fits into this but I don't really like the very roundabout reference.

Also, when referencing 'the Foundation' they used 'SCP' instead and I found that a bit annoying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that it has a title of sorts ('Untitled' is practically a placeholder, and the author should totally bother to give it a name),1 I wonder if anyone would make a meta Tale about its namelessness.

Footnotes
1. It's like giving birth and not bothering to name the kid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Monoliths are back, and now have greater coverage than Verizon or AT&T combined. Sign up now for NO COST DOWN.

I love the idea that this is basically 2001: A Space Odyssey, Brought To You By T-Mobile.

+1

item.title: [Idiocy Collapsed- Mgmt.]
item.content:encoded: Scintillating post removed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How about "Get with the times" or something to that effect

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why not name it "Pretty Fly for a WiFi"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[DATA EXPUNGED]

Kekekekedkekekeddkahjs

I am starting to get impatient. This SCP is a great one. Why is there STILL no title?

item.title: Re: SORRY!!!
item.content:encoded: Relax. We can revert it.

item.title: SORRY!!!
item.content:encoded: OMG!, I AM SO SORRY!!! I CHANGED THE NAME ON THE WRONG ONE!!! OMG I AM SO SORRY!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this still not have a name?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
STILL NO NICKNAME!

KeK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When will this get a name?
kek

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: add a title!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why doesn't this have a name on the mainlist…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It has been weeks and this Scip still has no nickname.

kek.

item.title: Jr. Staff Post
item.content:encoded: KingFlamestorm325, instead of replying to your own comments, edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just my honest opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How would you feel about an SCP that hinged on getting a detail of that thing very wrong? How easily would you just let it go?

Pretty easily, considering that I suck at SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
May i give you an idea for what to call this thing?

The Hotspot's Hotspots

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It wasn't a wifi hotspot until the scp foundation started pretending that it was? how does that work

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. +1

There's a typo in here: SCI-3993-1 instead of SCP-3993-1. Furthermore:

despite its apparently lack of menace

"apparently" should be "apparent". The former is an adverb whereas an adjective is appropriate.

I don't know whether I should fix this myself or ask for permission. I'm kinda new to actually contributing to this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Doomcard Ten. Lets use LinkNYC as it's nickname!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. I 100% support anyone trying to find new ways of conveying a story, and the great thing is that this isn't even a format screw. It's just a document written in a more digestable and engaging format.

+1, but it needs a mainlist title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's the monolith from 2001: A Space Odyssey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel that "LinkNYC" would probably be a good nickname for this. It's a short but appropriate nickname, like those given to some of the original SCP's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It had one. It was a bit unfitting, though, so I can see the need for changing it… Albeit it might've been better to come up with one before removing the old one, unless this is some mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this SCP ever going to be given a nickname? Like on the Series 4 list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tone is… Odd. Not very cold, flat observation like SCPs are usually written. I feel like the whole… "Bettering people" thing is just jammed in there, with little use or reason. It could stand alone as anomalously appearing WiFi signals. Also, the exchange with the D class was odd. D-class aren't let free, are they…? Maybe I'm wrong on this, but are they given the chance to like, go to school and whatnot? I'm sure it could be put away as "he still thinks he's being let out at the end of the month". All in all, while the tone is a bit off and parts of it could use some work, it's a novote rather than a downvote because, like Decibelles said, it was quite entertaining to read. As much as it strays from the typical SCP in terms of how it's written, I suppose it was like a breath of fresh, strange-scented air.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Torn on voting because the tone is pretty wild in places and it commits my actual single biggest pet peeve (putting the history before any description of what it does), but you managed to write it in a way where… I wasn't bothered by it. And in fact, it was entertaining to read. I think that's extremely rare. I don't know if you did so intentionally or not, but it works in this one instance. So take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm actually going to commend the author for breaking with the conventional SCP flow to more effectively tell a story. The pacing is excellent, and basically foregoing a list of detailed observations lets the piece provide elaborations and revelations as necessary while putting the history of the phenomenon, i.e. the real meat of the article, front and center. Avoiding a clear Description also really drives home that the Foundation is in the dark, with their hands tied.

Separating it into Description and History sections would stifle the piece, I think. I don't think many authors, if they tried to write this, would have the vision to realize that it needed to be written this way.

item.title: Re: Image source
item.content:encoded: Perfect, thanks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback everyone - I agree with a lot of it, and I think there are good ways to improve it. I thought I'd covered most of my bases with this SCP but clearly there are issues with the style (e.g. it reads too much like 'this happened, then that happened' etc.)

item.title: Image source
item.content:encoded: This image is CC-BY-3.0 Jim.henderson, via https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:3d_Av_16_St_LinkNYC_station_jeh.JPG

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fun fact, I work across the street from where this supposedly is.

Cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It needs a title. othersie, It is good, but not enough to make me +1. I am not going to -1 either.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page.

EDIT: Image is okay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tone is way off, and it takes far too long to get to what these are and what they do. This needs to be rewritten from the ground up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that's easy to say when reading about a topic that we don't understand well enough to know for sure when an SCP is doing it wrong.

Is there anything you care about and consider yourself well-versed in? How would you feel about an SCP that hinged on getting a detail of that thing very wrong? How easily would you just let it go?

I mean, shit, for what it's worth, Eol Kredik still upvoted the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my god, it's fine man. One detail doesn't automatically ruin the work.

item.title: Sort of like an advertisement
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy this idea but so many allusions to "LinkNYC being the best form of public Wi-Fi but anomalously" makes it reads like an advertisement for LinkNYC. The mentions of its "impressiveness" and "life changing content" really hamfists it. The stories also read like "wowee this FREE Internet service is changing my life for the BETTER!"

It's really well written but the subject matter isn't convincing. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-1 from me, I'm afraid.

As Accelerando mentioned, there's no actual description. Worse, it just continuously blurts the whole story out, one beat after another. It really doesn't fit clinical tone or the SCP format, but more importantly, it just isn't that interesting to read.

I think the real world references also took me out of it, but they probably wouldn't have been as bad if they weren't in the long litany of "and then this happened and then this happened and then this happened…"

You've gotta have some structure to your story. Everything the Foundation does here falls, but it fails pretty much the sentence after you mention it and then you plow right on, so it never comes as a twist or builds tension. (The "comedic" tone of the Foundation documents really did not help that.) All they ever discover is that it's more and more powerful. There's no hint to motive beyond… altruism maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the plus side, the story is clever and leaves me wanting more. An anomaly that actively services the public with something that everyone wants, becoming a norm before the Foundation can contain it. I also admit that the final note generated on each pillar got me; letting the reader hear the note as an advertisement rather than something like "We are coming" is greatly unsettling. This article makes an attempt at a narrative I haven't seen recently, and it deserves credit for that.

On the downside, the way this article is formatted is actually just a long Discovery: log, instead of a Description: . Because of this, there is no actual breakdown of what SCP-3993 is.

I'm settling on a novote for now.

Edit: If the ratings linger, I sincerely hope you consider rewriting this, author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While what you say is true, a neutrino tunneling through a Faraday cage being the source of a wifi connection is what makes no sense.

The "neutrino-based quantum tunneling effect" isn't the problem: it's the use of it. Faraday cages don't pose any more of an obstruction to a neutrino than air (over this distance, at least), and the idea that neutrinos can somehow interact enough to connect to a cell phone just doesn't hold.

If it was some type of novel particle, that'd be enough to handwave it, but even if the Foundation has some anomalous particle detector, the pragmatics alone make the neutrino theory shaky.

It's like if I started talking about superstring theory when trying to explain how a rubber ball bounces higher each time. It isn't incorrect by itself, but its application is the problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, how its based on this internet device that appear on every street (especially on 3rd ave)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its handwavey, but its not meaningless - that's one of the theories for how Neutrino Oscillation works; tunneling makes them flip to a different type.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of wi-fi things popping up with a sinister purpose but I never felt like I really had a good idea of what was going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I quite enjoyed this SCP, there was one line which, ah, stood out:

neutrino-based quantum tunnelling effect

This doesn't work — not in any context I can think of. Personally, I'd just say that the wifi was still available for unknown reasons, and leave it at that.
